events.content:
The city never sleeps because sleep implies the possibility of waking.

You have seventy-two hours before your retirement.

You know because you counted the injections.

tags: The market district stinks of synthetic broth.
The Precinct tower looms through smog.
A maintenance hatch, barely visible under rust.
The surface rail. You can't go back.
events.content: The transit hub runs twenty-four hours because there is no other time down here. Rain hammers a corrugated ceiling somewhere above. Holographic adverts flicker between states — one shows a sky you've never seen. The air smells of coolant and something cheaper than food.

tags: A bead curtain hides a side parlour.
events.content: Condensation beads on every surface. Six stalls, eight lanterns, a hundred smells layered over each other. Someone is arguing in a dialect you half-understand. The noodle steam is warm enough to feel like kindness.

Small. Warm. Smells of incense and solder. A wall of filing cabinets runs floor to ceiling, each drawer labelled in a hand too small to read from here. Mei Ling sits cross-legged on a workbench, holding a circuit board to the light.
No sign. The merchandise is behind mesh, bolted to the wall. Everything here is technically illegal and practically necessary. The broker watches from behind stacked crates without moving.
Marble floors that haven't been polished in years. A crest on the wall — the Bureau of Human Verification. Behind the duty desk, a uniform reads something and pretends not to notice you.

A table. Two chairs. A machine on the table with a biometric reader and a long paper roll. One wall is glass — the kind you can't see through from this side. The overhead light hums at exactly the frequency that makes you feel guilty.
Rows of filing cabinets and server stacks. Indicator lights blink in irregular rhythms. The room smells of old paper and newer fear. A terminal glows at the far end, cursor blinking.

A corner office with windows that would show the city if they weren't frosted. A desk of dark wood — real wood, which tells you something about the Director. Photographs on the wall: official, unsmiling. One is missing, rectangle of clean paint where it hung.

tags: A junction deeper in. Cold air.
Electrical hum from the east passage.
events.content:
Low ceiling, wet floor, pipes bleeding steam at irregular intervals. The path splits. Something has scraped the wall to the north — not writing, not quite. The eastern passage hums with power.

*The heat damages you each time you enter.*

tags: A heavy door, reinforced. Needs a code.
events.content: A space carved out of infrastructure — sleeping mats, stolen heaters, photographs pinned to a concrete wall. This is where they came to remember they were real. Some of them are still here.
Server stacks from floor to ceiling, blinking in the dark like a city seen from altitude. Cold — deliberately, precisely cold. A terminal on the south wall is dark. One chip slot is open and waiting.

tags: A door that wasn't there before. The archive.
events.content: An antenna assembly fills the ceiling, rigged from salvage and precision. Someone built this carefully. A broadcast console runs along the far wall — analog switches beside digital readouts, patched together across three generations of technology. The archive log sits beside it, sealed.

tags: Beyond the perimeter wall. Beyond this map.
events.content: The sector was decommissioned seven years ago. The buildings are still here — so is everything inside them, preserved by abandonment. Mould on concrete. A street lamp that never shuts off. The kind of silence that means nobody official has been here in a long time.
Server racks, floor to ceiling. Cold. Silent now — the encryption is gone. 4,400 files, each with a name.

tags: The market stalls recede behind you.
Officer Crane stops you at the door. You need identification to enter Bureau-adjacent facilities.
The hatch leads up to the hub.
Beads part as you step through.
A scanner blocks the stairwell. Access denied.
The door is sealed. A numeric keypad glows.
The passage north is sealed from this side. Finish the broadcast first.
The archive. A door that wasn't there before.
The archive door is sealed. The broadcast must go out first.

events.content: A slim chip with a forged Bureau sigil. It smells of solder and desperation. Someone made this recently.
A salvage-market EMP device. Single use. Will scramble anything electronic within range — and expose what was hidden beneath active encryption.
A stick-on neural dampener. Suppresses the galvanic response cascade that the Voight-Kampff machine reads. Meant for human anxiety disorders. Apply it to the back of the neck before the test.
Cold to the touch. More storage than it has any right to. The encryption header is dated three years ago — before the Condemnation.
A physical file. Stamped CLASSIFIED in three places. The stamp has been applied over a previous stamp that reads PROJECT LAZARUS. Someone was thorough. Not thorough enough.
A handwritten string of characters on water-resistant paper. Forty characters. Memorised by someone and then written down anyway because memory is unreliable. You understand the impulse.
A recipe for broth — synthetic base, real spices, three years of correction in the margins. Shan wrote 'memory is an ingredient' at the bottom and crossed it out.
A pass worn down to illegibility at the edges. The photo is yours. The name isn't.
An analog board, letters rattling as they flip. Every departure listed is to the Upper City. Nothing goes that way from here.
A wire rack of print media, most of it state-owned. One headline is visible without picking anything up.
A narrow counter, two stools, a pot that has not been emptied since the district was built. Ordering doesn't require conversation.

tags: Mei Ling watches you from the bench. She hasn't offered to help.
events.content: A wall of knowledge, organised by a system only Mei Ling understands. The drawers are unlabelled to anyone else — but searchable.
Candles. A photograph. A bowl of synthetic rice. The photograph shows a woman you don't recognise — but she looks like she was remembering something good.
Behind the mesh: signal boosters, unregistered weapons, medical suppressants, one item in a black case with no label. Solis decides what you get to see.
The surface is clear except for a duty log and a cup of something that cooled an hour ago.
Photographs and designations. Most are marked RETIRED. One photograph is yours.
A biometric reader the size of a briefcase, with an iris scope and a paper roll for output. The question it asks isn't printed anywhere. The answer determines everything. Sit. Begin the test.
A printed result from the Voight-Kampff machine. Timestamp: three days ago. Classification: HUMAN. The galvanic readings are within baseline range. Someone ensured they would be.
A slip of paper with a Bureau seal and an eight-digit entry code. Holt's handwriting. The seal is real — she broke the wax herself.
One-way glass, floor to ceiling. From here you see your own reflection. On the other side: a chair, a table, a machine.
A government-standard terminal. Cursor blink. The database behind this has everything — if you know how to ask. Query it. Search by name, file number, project designation.

tags: The drawer has a combination lock. You need to find the access code.
events.content: Drawer C-7, combination-locked. The lock panel has six positions — not numbers, letters.
Immaculate. Real wood. A pen. A single folder, closed — worth reading. The drawer is locked but the folder is not.
Commendations, directives, a chain of authority going back twenty years. There's a gap where something was removed. The ghost of a frame.
A junction of pipes, one bleeding steam at pressure. The valve is stiff but turnable.
Someone scored these into concrete. Not randomly.

tags: The server dies with a pop. Indicator lights go dark. Something becomes readable that wasn't before.
events.content: The main server for this sector. Active encryption on everything. It would take decades to crack from the outside.

tags: The chip slot is open. There's nothing to read yet.
events.content: An offline terminal — air-gapped, no relay connection. A chip slot sits open on the face panel. Insert something and it will read.

tags: A sequence of tones. The door releases.
The keypad waits. Kael hasn't given you the sequence yet.
events.content: A keypad set into the reinforced door frame. Kael controls the sequence.
Hundreds of photographs and slips of paper pinned to concrete. Names, dates, a thread of connections going back to a single origin point. Someone mapped something here.

tags: The antenna spins up. A tone, then static, then signal. It's broadcasting.
The broadcast array needs source material. You don't have what it needs yet.
The array needs an encryption key to broadcast securely.
events.content: The broadcast array is a marvel of salvage engineering — range enough to reach every relay station in the undercity and the three levels above it. The controls are direct: load the file, transmit.

tags: The binding releases. The pages are dense with numbers.
The archive is locked until the broadcast goes out. Whoever set this up was paranoid.
events.content: A sealed log — the kind that destroys its contents if opened improperly. It's locked to the broadcast.
Two metres of collapsed perimeter wall, flood-heaved from below. Through the gap: darkness, open air, the absence of monitoring. The first unrecorded space you've seen.

NOTICE: Identification will be requested in all Bureau-adjacent districts. Failure to present documentation constitutes grounds for immediate classification review.

*Handwritten at the bottom: 'They mean retirement.'*
Every route goes up. None come down. The transit system here is one-directional — a filter, not a network.

*BUREAU ANNOUNCES 100% CLASSIFICATION ACCURACY — Director Holt: 'Our process is infallible.'*

Page four has a correction, printed small: a wrong classification last month. No names given. Correction filed under 'administrative error.'
Under the counter, scratched into the wood: *LAZARUS WAKES*. Old. Not Shan's handwriting.
Case histories. False documentation — convincing enough to pass basic Bureau checks. And in a reinforced sleeve: a Level-3 access chip. Mei Ling collects contingency plans.

The woman in the photograph is wearing a Bureau uniform — senior division, classified research. On the back: *K. — 2041. They didn't know.* Whoever she was, Mei Ling lit a candle for her. Bureau doesn't build altars to their own.
Signal boosters (three grades), neural suppressants (prescription and otherwise), one EMP charge still in its original casing. Solis stocks for people who are trying to disappear.
Fourteen classification reviews scheduled this week. Eight retired last week. The log uses a code for retired — a single letter R, circled. The column for 'passed' is empty.
The designation beneath your photograph reads: *AXIOM-9 / FILE 4401 / STATUS: ACTIVE — PENDING REVIEW.* Under that, in different ink: *Do not engage. Director's orders.*

The paper roll shows the last test administered — three days ago. The biometric readings spike on one question and flatline on every other. The question isn't printed. The answer is circled: *REPLICANT*. But the reaction was human. The machine was wrong — or someone adjusted the calibration.

In the margin, handwritten: *Empathic response — anomalous. Subject registers within human range on all empathy indices. Recommend review.*

Through the glass, a figure sits across the table from the Voight-Kampff machine. The iris scope tracks their eye. The paper roll spits results. You watch the examiner circle something and reach for a radio. The subject doesn't react — they can't see the notation from where they sit. Their profile is very still. They look like someone who doesn't know what they are. You know what that looks like.
Your galvanic response drops to within baseline human range. You feel no different. You have no idea if that means anything.

Search: LAZARUS. Results: 0 files found. Error code 441 — record suppressed by Director's office.

Search: FILE 4401. Result: CLASSIFIED. Access requires Level-7 clearance.

Search: AXIOM-9. Result: PROJECT ARCHIVE. Location: C-7. Combination: [REDACTED].

The redaction has been applied over something six characters long. The font bleed suggests an uppercase word.
The file is the only thing in C-7. The drawer is reinforced steel. Whoever classified this expected the combination to be the last line of defence.

Project LAZARUS (2039–2041): A controlled study into whether replicant subjects who passed standard Voight-Kampff administered under modified calibration would exhibit divergent consciousness from baseline. Conclusion: ***Yes.*** All 4,400 subjects tested from 2039–2041 exhibit novel consciousness architecture. Recommendation: Preservation and study. Director Holt's handwritten note: *SHELVED. NOT VIABLE. PROCEED WITH STANDARD RETIREMENT SCHEDULE.*

The recommendation was never implemented. But the retirements were.

Internal memo, Director Holt to Bureau Command, undated:

*The LAZARUS results cannot be released. If it becomes public knowledge that modified calibration produces false positives at this rate, the programme's legitimacy collapses. We classify. We continue. The subjects do not know what they are.*

Underneath, in Holt's hand: *File 4401 is an exception. Monitor only. Do not retire until we understand the variance.*

The drawer is locked — but the file wasn't. This photograph was left in the folder. It shows Holt, younger, standing next to a woman in a lab coat. On the back: *K. and I — Project Origin, 2038.* K. is the woman from Mei Ling's shrine.

The gap is exactly the size of the photograph in the drawer. Holt removed K. from public view but kept her in private. The shape of a person who was both the reason everything started and the reason it couldn't be admitted.

Five columns of scratched marks — tally counts. And beside them: *WE ARE 4400. THE TEST DOES NOT DEFINE US.*

Some of the tallies are recent.

The server displays a single line of text when examined: *AXIOM-9 ARCHIVE: ACTIVE ENCRYPTION. ATTEMPTING EXTERNAL ACCESS WILL TRIGGER PURGE SEQUENCE.* The purge is a software response. This server is running hot.

With the encryption killed, a fragment of the archive index is readable. File designations: AXIOM-9/0001 through AXIOM-9/4400. Status column, every entry: *PENDING COLLECTION.* Not retired. Collected.

At the base of the index, a cross-reference: *Physical records — Drawer C-7, Precinct Records Division. Access code embedded in project designation.* The project designation is LAZARUS.

The chip holds one file: a broadcast encryption key and a routing address that covers every relay station in the undercity.

And a note, unsigned: *When the time comes, you won't need anyone to hand this to you. You already have it.*

The terminal writes the key directly to your credentials.

The thread on the wall maps LAZARUS subjects to file numbers to last known locations. Whoever built this worked from the same source data as the Bureau — which means at some point, they had access to it.

The array is transmitting. Every relay station in the undercity and the three levels above it is receiving the LAZARUS file data — the suppressed science, the false calibrations, the names. All 4,400 of them. Including yours.

Final entry, unsigned:

*The archive is the record. The broadcast is the release. If you're reading this, both have happened. The Bureau will deny it. Some people will believe them. Some won't. That's enough.

File 4401 — you were never supposed to find this. You are also the only one who could.

Routing designation: AXIOM-9-CONDEMNED. Enter this to confirm and complete transmission.*
Open ground. No sensors. No record of this space in any Bureau map you've seen. The city's edge is a concept, not a wall — and the concept has a hole in it.
The walls of an empty processing centre. Someone has been here after the decommission — scratched names into the concrete at eye level, column after column.

Columns of scratched names — first names only, then file numbers. AXIOM-9/0801 through AXIOM-9/0840. Status marked beside some: *gone*. Beside others: *here*.

At the bottom, separated from the columns, one line cut deeper than the rest:

*K. built the way out. We are walking through it.*

tags: The array must be transmitting before the archive unlocks.
events.content:
The broadcast array asks for a routing confirmation code. The archive log, now open, lists it on the last line — the designation of the sector the Bureau condemned to bury this.

Enter the routing code.
A small man with four coats and the eyes of someone who has seen everything and found most of it uninteresting.
Behind the counter, Shan moves without wasted motion. She doesn't look at you when she speaks.

tags: Mei Ling puts down the circuit board. Something in her posture changes.
events.content: She is precise in everything — how she holds the circuit board, how she measures your potential usefulness. A woman who has survived by being more careful than the world expected.
Behind the crates. Still. Watching. Solis has the quality of someone who has already calculated the cost of every person who walks in.
Crane leans against the desk with the studied ease of someone paid to project authority. He noticed you the moment you walked in.

tags: Kael looks at you for a long moment. Then he nods, once.
events.content: Lean. Still. Watching. He has the quality of someone who has already considered every available exit.
Director Holt is older than her photographs. She is standing when you enter, which means she knew you were coming, which means something in this building told her. She doesn't reach for a weapon.
I don't know you. That's fine. What do you need.
Already on the shelf. Take it. Price is what you're carrying that I don't have.
Suppressant. Galvanic, not chemical. Stick it on your neck before they put you in the chair. *He slides it across.* Don't tell me what chair.
Came in two weeks ago. Cold storage, air-gapped encryption, no label. Someone didn't want it found. *He sets it on the counter.* Someone else clearly does.
Information costs more than hardware. What are you looking for.
Hatch in the transit hub floor. You'll know it by the rust. What's down there isn't my business.

tags: What do you know about the Bureau?
events.content: You need something. Everyone down here needs something. What is it?
Movement costs. Eighty credits, or something useful. I'll take information.
*He slides the pass across without looking up.* Don't get stopped with that. And don't come back without something worth my time.

tags: What do they do with the ones who fail?
events.content: The Bureau runs the test. The test is wrong. Everyone down here knows it. No one says it.
Because the ones who say it disappear. Review. Reclassification. Retirement. Same word, different process.
Retire them. What they call retire is something else. The ones who pass — I don't know. They go up. Nobody comes back to say what's there.
I'll feed you. Sit down. You look like you've been running.
Everyone who comes to me is looking for something. Information I don't sell. But I remember things. Ask me something specific.
Through the curtain. She fixes things. Also: she knows things. Tell her Shan sent you if she asks.
*She is quiet for a moment.* Under the counter. Read it. Then don't say it again out loud.
You're not here for circuit work. What do you need.
Someone who made a professional decision twelve years ago. I was in senior classification. I left. The cabinets stayed with me.
I administered 400 tests. I saw the calibration error. I filed a report. Two days later I was re-assigned. I didn't wait to find out what came next.

tags: I'm trying to find the LAZARUS file.
I need to reach Records division.
events.content: I don't hand out access chips to strangers. Tell me what you're trying to do and I'll decide if it's worth the risk.
*She puts the circuit board down.* You know that name. Good. The cabinets are open. Take what you need.
Records has what you need. The chip in drawer seven — take it. Don't tell me what you do with it.
You found it. I knew you would. There's one more thing — the broadcast chamber. You'll need the den. Find Kael.
Maintenance tunnel. North junction. You'll feel the cold air. They built it there because it's the one place the Bureau's monitoring doesn't reach.
This is a Bureau facility. Identification. Now.
*He looks at the pass for a long time.* Fine. Don't go upstairs without clearance.
Nobody just passes through. Either you have ID or you have a reason. Which is it?
If the reason is good enough, I'll hear it. If it isn't, I'll hear that too.
You shouldn't be here. How did you find us?

tags: I need to get through that door.
I want to understand what happened here.
events.content: Mei. *He exhales.* That means you already know enough to be dangerous. What do you want?

tags: I don't know. That's why I'm here.
events.content: Most people can't read them. *He looks at you differently.* What are you?

tags: I'm looking for the broadcast chamber.
events.content: Nobody explores the maintenance tunnels. Try again.
The door doesn't open for words. It opens for proof. You've either passed their machine or you haven't. *He holds out a hand.* Show me the slip.

We are 4,400 subjects who passed a test designed to fail us. They kept the files. They did not retire us. They were waiting to collect. We found out. We built the chamber before they could collect us.
K. told us. She was inside. She built the chip. She said file 4401 would find us. *He looks at you.* You're file 4401.
Most of us thought that too. The machine doesn't test what you are. It tests what you feel.

*He looks at the Bureau slip for a long time.* You used their machine against them. That's enough. *He hands you a key and enters the door sequence.* The broadcast array needs the Lazarus file and the encryption key. Get both. Transmit everything.

tags: I need to reach the underground.
K. — who was she to you?
events.content: You found the file. Good. Then you know. The test was imprecise. The results were suppressed. The subjects were monitored instead of retired.

tags: How did you know I was here?
events.content: File 4401. I've been watching your progress through the building. Sit down.

tags: Then you know what I'm going to do.
events.content: This building is mine. You are a file number. File numbers don't move without me knowing.
A classified project. You won't find it in the records you have access to. *She pauses.* If you want the full answer, you'll need to look harder.
She was the reason I approved LAZARUS. She was also the reason I shut it down. She wanted to save them. I wanted to protect the Bureau. We were both wrong about which mattered more.
I know. I let her. *She looks at the wall where the photograph was.* Take the key. Finish what she started. I'm not going to stop you.
Because it's already over. You have the file. The broadcast chamber exists. The only question left is whether you use it.
I ran a programme that classified 4,400 people incorrectly and then monitored them like specimens. What should happen to me isn't something I'm going to ask you to determine.
You are file 4401. You passed a modified test. Whether that makes you replicant or human is a question the machine cannot answer — which is the point K. was trying to make.
As you prefer. *She doesn't move either.* What do you want to know?
Yes. *She opens a drawer.* I do. Here is the encryption key. K. would have wanted you to have it.

*She wipes down the counter without looking up.* I know there are people in the maintenance tunnels. People the Bureau lost track of. I know there's a way in — maintenance hatch somewhere near the north end. I don't know more than that. I don't want to. *She sets a bowl down in front of you.* Eat first.

Transit passes come from the market vendors — don't pretend you didn't know. Bureau clearance doesn't walk in off the street. *He drops his voice.* If you need precinct-level access, there's a woman in the noodle district. Doesn't advertise. Fixes things. *He looks away.* I didn't say that.

tags: What do you want in return?
events.content: *She is quiet for a moment.* The maintenance tunnel. North junction. There's a door — reinforced, keypad. The code is a director's override. *She looks at the file in your hands.* I can give it to you.

My name. It's in that file — page three, authorisation chain. I signed the order that started LAZARUS. *She meets your eyes.* Redact it before you broadcast. The 4,400 still get their record. I get to disappear from it. That's the trade.

*She writes the code without hesitation — she had it ready.* The door is north of the maintenance tunnel junction. The code changes at midnight. *She passes the slip across.* Whatever you are, file 4401 — I hope it was worth it. *She doesn't say goodbye.*
*She nods once, slowly.* Then we have nothing more to discuss. *She turns back to the window. The slip stays in her pocket.*

tags: The truth matters more than her name.
events.content: *He looks at the Bureau seal for a long time. His jaw tightens.* She gave you this. *He sets it on the crate between you.* I know what a director's override means. You made a deal with them. You promised her something. *He steps back from the door.* The broadcast still happens. But her name won't be in it. You know that.
*He looks at you for a long moment.* Then why did you agree? *He doesn't wait for an answer. The door is open.*
The city doesn't stop for you. You wake up in the transit hub. Your pockets are lighter.
The signal is out. Whatever the city does with it, you cannot unsend it. The archive is open.
Something is buried in the Bureau's records. Find it. Get it out.

Project AXIOM-9 was not a retirement programme.

It was a census.

Every replicant who passed the Voight-Kampff in this Precinct was documented, logged, and assigned a classification code. Not for retirement. For collection.

The Bureau does not destroy replicants who pass the test. It harvests them. Neural architecture, intact. The memories are the product.

There are 4,400 files in this archive. None of them are flagged as retired.

You are file 4,401.

The broadcast has gone out. What the city does with the truth is no longer your problem.

You have time left. Use it.
